# Data validations

# Investigate the output file after fragmentation

::: {#2b828caa .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Load the new output file after fragmentation
import pandas as pd
# complete fragemented
complete_fragmented_df = pd.read_csv("../result/complete_fragmented/eris_complete_fragmented_report.tsv", sep="\t")

# Complete
complete_df = pd.read_csv("../result/complete_genome/eris_output_complete_report.tsv", sep="\t")
complete_counts = complete_df.groupby("target")["target"].count() 

# Simulated
simulated_df = pd.read_csv("../result/output_simulated/eris_output_simulated_report.tsv", sep="\t")
simulated_counts = simulated_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()
```
:::


Want to see how much number of IS elements are detected 

::: {#7c061229 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Check the dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation  
print("Dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation:", complete_fragmented_df.shape)
# Group IS elements together and count the number of occurrences for each IS element in the new dataframe
complete_fragmented_counts = complete_fragmented_df.groupby("target")["target"].count()
print("Total IS element counts in the new dataframe after fragmentation:", complete_fragmented_counts.count())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Dimension of the new dataframe after fragmentation: (212, 9)
Total IS element counts in the new dataframe after fragmentation: 24
```
:::
:::


## If they are intersecting

::: {#ad8d108c .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Check if the IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly are also found in the simulated
common_fragmented = set(complete_fragmented_counts.index) & set(simulated_counts.index)
print("Number of common IS elements found in both datasets after fragmentation:", len(common_fragmented))
print("IS elements found in both datasets after fragmentation:")
print(common_fragmented)

# Check the IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data
unique_fragmented = set(complete_fragmented_counts.index) - set(simulated_counts.index)
print("Number of IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data:", len(unique_fragmented))
print("IS elements found in the fragmented complete genome assembly that are not found in the simulated data:")
print(unique_fragmented)
```

## Total disruption

::: {#1fa49476 .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def calc_disruption(df):
    total = int((df['effect'] == 'DISRUPTED').sum())
    percentage = float(total / len(df) *100)
    return total, round(percentage, 3)

print("Total IS that is disrupted in complete genome:", calc_disruption(complete_df))
print("Total IS that is disrupted in fragmented genome:", calc_disruption(complete_fragmented_df))
print("Total IS that is disrupted in simulated genome:", calc_disruption(simulated_df))

```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Total IS that is disrupted in complete genome: (7, 18.919)
Total IS that is disrupted in fragmented genome: (24, 11.321)
Total IS that is disrupted in simulated genome: (16, 5.461)
```
:::
:::

: Demonstration of pipe table syntax

# Do the precision and recall calculations for the fragmented genome assembly

I have confusion how to calculate the precision and recall.

In the .bed file provided from Genbank, there are multiple IS with unique identifiers. We need to ensure we predicted the right IS element. 

We group the IS elements and count the number of occurrences for each IS element in the new dataframe. We also want to separate the name as well to identify the location.

::: {#725adf20 .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# obtain the first IS name
complete_fragmented_counts = complete_fragmented_df.groupby("target")["target"].count().reset_index(name="count")

complete_fragmented_counts["Name"] = complete_fragmented_counts['target'].str.split('_').str[0]

# Make it as a new variables (a bit too long)
clean_frag = complete_fragmented_counts
```
:::


## Reading the BED file
Notice the position is so different. Since we want to intersect with the bed file, we want to have the original position back from the complete sequence

::: {#2fb52294 .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Reading the bed file
bed_columns = ['transcript', 'chromStart', 'chromEnd', 'name', 'score', 'strand']

# Read the file (use only the names that match your file's column count)
ann_bed = pd.read_table('../../data/complete_ec958_IS.reanotated.sorted.bed', header=None, names=bed_columns[:6])

print(ann_bed) 
```

## Calculate it based on the names
---

::: {#00afb3bd .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Make a list for the name only and separate the underscore 
# We remove any types in different position
ref_list = list(ann_bed["name"].str.split('_').str[0].drop_duplicates())

print(f'Total IS found in the list{len(ref_list)}')
print(ref_list)

ref_set = set(ref_list)

# Flag each row whether it's found or not
clean_frag['correct'] = clean_frag['Name'].isin(ref_set)

# Overall accuracy
accuracy_frag = clean_frag['correct'].mean()
print(f'Accuracy is {accuracy_frag:.2%}')

# Names not predicted in the list
wrong_frag = clean_frag.loc[~clean_frag['correct'], 'Name'].unique()
print("Names not found in reference list:", wrong_frag)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
